title: Data Types & Operations
description: An in-depth look at Python's bitwise operations, implicit/explicit type casting, and advanced memory representation patterns.

sections.title: Bitwise Operators in Detail
sections.description: Bitwise operators perform calculations on the binary digits (bits) of integers. Python simulates an infinite two's complement system for integers, meaning there is no sign-bit limit on left shifts, unlike fixed-width platforms.
sections.codeBlock:
a = 60            # 60 = 0011 1100 in binary
b = 13            # 13 = 0000 1101 in binary

# Bitwise AND (&) - 1 if both bits are 1
bitwise_and = a & b
print(bitwise_and) # 12 (0000 1100)

# Bitwise OR (|) - 1 if at least one bit is 1
bitwise_or = a | b
print(bitwise_or)  # 61 (0011 1101)

# Bitwise XOR (^) - 1 if bits are different
bitwise_xor = a ^ b
print(bitwise_xor) # 49 (0011 0001)

# Bitwise NOT (~) - inverts all bits (equal to -(x+1))
bitwise_not = ~a
print(bitwise_not) # -61 (twos complement representation)

# Bitwise Left Shift (<<) - shifts bits left, filling with zeros
left_shift = a << 2
print(left_shift)  # 240 (1111 0000)

# Bitwise Right Shift (>>) - shifts bits right, dropping rightmost
right_shift = a >> 2
print(right_shift) # 15 (0000 1111)

sections.title: Implicit vs. Explicit Type Casting
sections.description: Python supports two types of casting: Implicit Conversion (automatic type promotion by the interpreter to prevent data loss) and Explicit Casting (forcing a type conversion using built-in constructors).
sections.codeBlock:
# Implicit Casting (Coercion)
integer_num = 100
float_num = 20.5
sum_val = integer_num + float_num
print(sum_val)        # 120.5 (integer is implicitly promoted to float)
print(type(sum_val))  # <class 'float'>

# Explicit Casting (Conversion)
string_digit = "123"
converted_int = int(string_digit)
print(converted_int)  # 123
print(type(converted_int)) # <class 'int'>

# Float to Int Casting (Truncation gotcha)
pi_val = 3.99
truncated_int = int(pi_val)
print(truncated_int)  # 3 (truncates decimal part, does not round)

# Boolean Evaluation (Truthiness)
print(bool(0))        # False
print(bool(0.0))      # False
print(bool(""))       # False (empty string is falsy)
print(bool([]))       # False (empty list is falsy)
print(bool([1, 2]))   # True

sections.title: Advanced Bitwise Tricks & Gotchas
sections.description: Practical applications of bit manipulation include bitmasking for flags, checking parity, fast multiplication/division, and simulating fixed-width integers using bit masks.
sections.codeBlock:
# 1. Bit Masking (Flags & Permissions)
FLAG_READ = 0b100    # 4
FLAG_WRITE = 0b010   # 2
FLAG_EXEC = 0b001    # 1

# Grant Read and Write permissions
current_perms = FLAG_READ | FLAG_WRITE
print(current_perms) # 6 (0b110)

# Verify if Write permission is present
has_write = (current_perms & FLAG_WRITE) != 0
print(has_write)     # True

# Revoke Write permission using XOR/AND-NOT
current_perms = current_perms ^ FLAG_WRITE
print(current_perms) # 4 (0b100, only Read left)

# 2. Check Even or Odd (Parity check)
num = 45
is_odd = (num & 1) != 0
print(is_odd)        # True (odd numbers end in 1 in binary)

# 3. Simulating 8-Bit Unsigned Integer Overflow
# Since Python integers do not overflow, use a mask to force 8-bit limits
MASK_8BIT = 0xFF     # 255 in decimal
raw_val = 250
overflow_val = (raw_val + 15) & MASK_8BIT
print(overflow_val)  # 9 (265 & 255 = 9)

# 4. High-Performance Shifting
x = 7
multiply_by_8 = x << 3
print(multiply_by_8) # 56 (equal to 7 * 2^3)
divide_by_4 = x >> 2
print(divide_by_4)   # 1 (equal to 7 // 2^2)

details:
CPython Integer Architecture: Unlike C or Java where integers are mapped directly to 32 or 64-bit CPU registers, Python's int is a C struct (PyLongObject) containing a digit array (typically using 30-bit digits). This enables arbitrary-precision math, but makes bitwise operations slightly more complex. When performing shifts or bitwise logical operations, Python dynamically simulates an infinite two's complement binary representation. Negative numbers are simulated as having an infinite sequence of leading 1 bits. This is why ~x is equivalent to -(x + 1).

Implicit Coercion Mechanics: In binary arithmetic expressions, Python enforces safety but implements a numeric promotion hierarchy. Operating on mixed types (e.g. int and float) promotes the operand lower on the hierarchy to the higher type (integer is promoted to float). Float operations follow the IEEE 754 double-precision standard. Note that implicit promotion does not occur between text sequences (str) and numeric types—attempting to add "5" + 5 raises a TypeError to avoid ambiguity.

Explicit Casting Gotchas: The int() constructor truncates floats toward zero (e.g. int(3.9) is 3, and int(-3.9) is -3). To round mathematically, use round() (which uses round-to-even for ties, e.g. round(2.5) == 2 and round(3.5) == 4). When casting custom classes, Python looks for dunder methods: __int__(), __float__(), and __bool__(). For truthiness, any object whose __bool__() returns False or whose __len__() returns 0 is falsy; all other objects default to truthy.

Advanced Bitmask Patterns: In systems programming, bitmasking stores configuration flags inside a single integer. The bitwise operators & (AND), | (OR), and ^ (XOR) let you inspect, set, and toggle permissions with high efficiency. Because Python integers do not overflow, simulating 8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit registers (e.g. for cryptography or low-level serial port emulations) requires masking variables manually (e.g. val & 0xFFFFFFFF for 32-bit unsigned integers) or using the ctypes module to enforce native C data type constraints.
